Question: Identify an object that undergoes uniform circular motion. Describe how you could trace the SHM of this object.
Answer: A ketchup bottle sits on a lazy Susan in the center of the dinner table. You set it rotating in uniform circular motion. A set of lights shine on the bottle, producing a shadow on the wall.
o3 Answer:
Example of an object with uniform circular motion  
Stick a small piece of reflective tape (or an LED) on the rim of a record-player/turn-table that is spinning at a constant speed.  The tape moves in a circle of fixed radius r with constant angular speed ω; that is uniform circular motion.

How to “extract’’ its simple harmonic motion (SHM)  
1. Let the turn-table lie in a horizontal plane.  
2. Place a vertical screen a short distance away and shine a bright lamp horizontally so that the piece of tape casts a sharp shadow on the screen.  
3. Watch only the horizontal (or vertical) position of that shadow—the shadow is the projection of the circular motion onto one diameter of the circle.  
4. Mark the shadow’s position on the screen (or film the shadow) at equal time intervals, or let the screen be a long strip of paper that is pulled past at a constant speed (as in a chart recorder).  

The recorded positions will oscillate back and forth between ±r, tracing a sine-wave pattern on the moving paper.  That sinusoidal trace is the simple harmonic motion associated with the original uniform circular motion; its amplitude is r and its angular frequency is the same ω as the turn-table.
o4-mini Answer:
One of the simplest realizations of SHM is just the one‐dimensional “shadow” of a point in uniform circular motion.  Here is a concrete example and how you would trace out its SHM:

1.  Choose your “circular‐motion” object  
    •  Paint or tape a small bright dot (or attach a tiny mirror) to the rim of a turntable, a bicycle wheel, or a disk on a motor shaft.  
    •  Drive the wheel at constant angular speed ω so that the dot moves in a perfect circle at radius R.

2.  Project out one coordinate (the SHM)  
    •  Shine a fixed lamp (or laser) at the dot so that it casts a shadow on a vertical screen (or wall) a few meters away.  
    •  As the wheel spins, the shadow slides back and forth in a straight horizontal line.

3.  Trace (or film) the shadow vs. time  
    •  Mark on the screen a strip of paper (or attach a ruler) parallel to the expected line of motion.  
    •  Either  
     –  Manually note the shadow’s horizontal position at equally spaced time intervals (using a stopwatch and marking ticks on the paper), or  
     –  Take a high‐speed video (or strobe‐photograph the shadow) and extract the x–t data afterwards.  

4.  Verify the sinusoid  
    •  If you plot the shadow’s horizontal displacement x versus time t, you will get  
       x(t) = R cos(ωt + φ₀),  
      which is exactly the defining equation of simple harmonic motion with amplitude R and angular frequency ω.

In this way, the one‐dimensional back‐and‐forth motion of the shadow is a direct physical realization of SHM, driven by the underlying uniform circular motion of your dot on the wheel.
deepseek-chat Answer:
### **Solution:**

**1. Identify an Object in Uniform Circular Motion:**  
A common example of an object undergoing **uniform circular motion (UCM)** is a **point on the edge of a spinning bicycle wheel** (assuming constant angular speed).

**2. Tracing Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) from UCM:**  
To trace the SHM of this object, we can project its motion onto a straight line (e.g., the x-axis or y-axis). Here’s how:

- **Visualization:** Imagine the spinning wheel is moving such that its plane is perpendicular to your line of sight.  
- **Projection:** Focus only on the **vertical (y-component)** or **horizontal (x-component)** motion of the point as the wheel rotates.  
  - If the wheel has radius \( A \) and angular velocity \( \omega \), the position of the point at time \( t \) is:  
    \[
    y(t) = A \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{(vertical SHM)}
    \]
    or  
    \[
    x(t) = A \cos(\omega t) \quad \text{(horizontal SHM)}.
    \]  
  - This sinusoidal motion is **SHM**, where \( A \) is the amplitude and \( \omega \) is the angular frequency.

**3. Practical Method to Trace SHM:**  
- **Shadow Projection:** Shine a light on the spinning wheel and observe the shadow of the point on a wall. The shadow will move up and down (or side-to-side) in SHM.  
- **Graphing:** Plot the vertical position \( y(t) = A \sin(\omega t) \) over time to see the SHM waveform.

**Conclusion:**  
By projecting the circular motion of a point on a spinning wheel onto a linear axis, we observe SHM. This demonstrates the deep connection between UCM and SHM.  

**Key Parameters:**  
- Amplitude (\( A \)) = Radius of the wheel.  
- Angular frequency (\( \omega \)) = Angular speed of the wheel.  
- Period (\( T \)) = \( \frac{2\pi}{\omega} \).  

This method is foundational in understanding wave motion, pendulums, and oscillatory systems in physics.
gemini-2.5-pro Answer:
Of course. Here is a solution and explanation for that problem.

### **Solution to Check Your Understanding 15.3**

This question asks us to connect two fundamental concepts in physics: Uniform Circular Motion (UCM) and Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM). The key idea is that **SHM is the one-dimensional projection of UCM.**

---

### **Part 1: Identify an object that undergoes uniform circular motion.**

An object is in **Uniform Circular Motion (UCM)** if it travels in a circular path at a constant speed.

**Object:** A horse on a spinning carousel (merry-go-round).

**Justification:**
*   **Circular Path:** The horse is fixed at a certain distance from the center and moves in a perfect circle as the carousel rotates.
*   **Uniform Speed:** A well-operated carousel rotates at a constant rate (constant angular velocity), meaning the horse on its edge moves at a constant speed.

Other excellent examples include:
*   The tip of the second hand of a clock.
*   A spot of paint on the blade of a fan spinning at a constant speed.
*   A point on the edge of a spinning vinyl record or CD.

### **Part 2: Describe how you could trace the SHM of this object.**

To trace the Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) of the carousel horse, we need to observe its motion from a one-dimensional perspective. The classic and most intuitive way to do this is by using a shadow.

Here is a step-by-step description of how to trace the SHM:

**The Setup (The Shadow Analogy):**

1.  **Light Source:** Imagine it's a sunny day and the sun is low in the sky (like at sunset), or we set up a very large, distant spotlight. The key is that the light rays striking the carousel are essentially parallel to each other.

2.  **The Object in UCM:** Our carousel with the horse is spinning at a constant speed.

3.  **The Screen:** A long, straight wall is positioned next to the carousel, acting as a screen. The wall is placed so the shadow of the horse is cast onto it.

**The Observation and Tracing:**

1.  As the carousel horse moves in its **two-dimensional circular path**, its shadow on the wall does **not** move in a circle.

2.  Instead, the shadow moves back and forth along a **one-dimensional straight line** on the wall.

3.  **Analyzing the Shadow's Motion:**
    *   When the horse is at the point in its circle closest to or farthest from the wall, its shadow on the wall is at the very ends of its path. At these two points, the shadow momentarily **stops** before reversing direction. These are the **amplitude** positions of the SHM.
    *   When the horse is moving directly towards or away from the wall (at the 3 o'clock and 9 o'clock positions, relative to the wall), its shadow is moving at its **maximum speed** through the center point of its path. This is the **equilibrium position** of the SHM.

**Conclusion:**

This back-and-forth (oscillatory) motion of the shadow is a perfect physical demonstration of **Simple Harmonic Motion**. By tracing the position of the shadow on the wall over time, you are tracing the SHM component of the horse's uniform circular motion. If you were to plot the shadow's position versus time, you would get a perfect sine or cosine wave, the mathematical hallmark of SHM.
gpt-5 Answer:
- Example of uniform circular motion: A point on the rim of a turntable (or a bicycle wheel) spinning at constant angular speed.

- How to trace its SHM: Observe the motion of that point’s projection onto a diameter. For instance, attach a small LED to the rim and cast its shadow onto a screen; the shadow’s horizontal (or vertical) position oscillates sinusoidally with amplitude equal to the radius and period equal to the rotation period. You can record this by letting the shadow mark a moving strip of paper, yielding a sine wave.
